lede: Three decisions recur in any non-trivial analysis. Each admits several defensible answers; the correct answer depends on the question and on the structure of the missingness or the join.

boxes.title: Missing values — drop rows, drop columns, fill, or allow to propagate?
boxes.intro: A column contains NaN. The decision is determined by two considerations: why the value is missing, and how essential the column is to the question.

options.label: Drop the row — dropna(subset=[col])
options.body: Indicated when the column is essential to the analysis (e.g. patient_id, admission_date) and no substitute is defensible. Acceptable if fewer than approximately five per cent of rows are lost.

options.label: Drop the column — drop(columns=[col])
options.body: Indicated when the column is more than approximately half missing and not load-bearing for the question, or is redundant with another column. The decision should be recorded explicitly in the notebook.

options.label: Impute — fillna(median) / ffill
options.body: Indicated when missingness is plausibly random, the column is numeric, and a substitute is defensible. The median is robust to skew; the mean only for symmetric distributions; forward-fill for time series whose state changes infrequently.

options.label: Allow to propagate
options.body: Indicated when the downstream aggregation is itself robust to NaN (pandas's mean, sum, and groupby skip NaN by default). Only when a NaN-propagating result is required does one specify skipna=False.

boxes.note: The decision should be made per column, not per DataFrame. dropna() applied to the whole DataFrame, with default arguments, is almost always the wrong choice on real data.

boxes.title: Merging two DataFrames — which join, which method?

options.label: Enrich table A with columns from B
options.body: . Retains every row of A; adds matching columns from B, with NaN where no match exists.

options.label: Restrict to rows present in both
options.body: . Smaller result; no missing values introduced by the merge.

options.label: Union of all keys
options.body: . Permits auditing of which keys are present in only one source.

options.label: A is the reference; B fills the gaps
options.body: . Applied cell by cell: where A has a value, A is retained; where A is NaN, B is used.

boxes.title: NumPy — which array-combination function?

options.label: Two 1D arrays → 2D with each as a column
Two 1D arrays → 2D with each as a row

boxes.note: Mnemonic: the argument axis denotes the dimension along which the result grows. axis=0 produces additional rows; axis=1 produces additional columns.
